prompt:
is this still an acurate description for the original issue ticket:
An SCP security review identified ~22 endpoints across 8 System area controllers missing
backend authorization checks. The Angular frontend restricts access by role, but the API
endpoints themselves don't enforce it — any user with a valid cert/FIDO token can call them
directly. Additionally, the 6 controllers that do have correct auth use inconsistent inline
patterns that are easy to forget when adding new methods.
This issue creates a reusable [RequirePrivilege] attribute that can be applied declaratively at the class or method level, then proves it works on ValidIssuerController as a reference
implementation. Subsequent issues (one per controller) will apply it across all System
controllers.
Problem
The codebase has two working auth patterns — AllowedAccessCheck() for app-scoped checks and
IsSuperAdmin() for role checks — but they weren't applied consistently. Developers have to
remember to add inline checks to every new method. When they forget, the endpoint is unprotected and the only thing stopping unauthorized access is the Angular UI, which is
trivially bypassed.
Solution
A single [RequirePrivilege] attribute with 4 modes covering every auth pattern in the System area:


        
      Role-based: [RequirePrivilege(RequiredRole.SuperAdmin)] — for controllers restricted to
specific roles (ValidIssuer, EmailTemplate, OnboardingApp, TeamsConsole, ServiceProviderRequest)

        
      Privilege-only: [RequirePrivilege(UserPrivilegeLevel.UserManagement)] — gates access for
users with a privilege level for at least one app (SupportApp)

        
      App-scoped via route: [RequirePrivilege(UserPrivilegeLevel.SystemManagement, ResourceParam.AppId)] — checks the user has a privilege for the specific app from the route
parameter (ApplicationManagement, ApplicationQuestion, QuestionValues, RoleExclusion)

        
      App-scoped via known app: [RequirePrivilege(UserPrivilegeLevel.HelpDeskPrivileges, KnownAppId.DPS)] — checks the user has a privilege for a fixed application (DPSTrustedAgent, GFMCoordinator)

All parameters are enums — zero magic strings. CertificateOrFIDO authentication is enforced
by default via IAuthorizationService delegation to the existing registered policy, with an
EnforceCertOrFido = false opt-out for the rare case (ClientSettings).
The attribute also respects [AllowAnonymous], supports custom denial logging via an optional IPrivilegeDenialHandler interface, and includes 8 guard-rail unit tests that auto-discover
controllers and catch misconfigurations as subsequent branches land.
Scope of this issue


        
      Create RequiredRole, KnownAppId, ResourceParam enums

        
      Create IPrivilegeDenialHandler interface

        
      Create RequirePrivilegeAttribute with 4 constructor overloads

        
      16+ unit tests covering all modes, edge cases, AllowAnonymous bypass, CertOrFIDO delegation, denial handler callback

        
      Apply to ValidIssuerController as proof of concept with reflection tests

        
      8 guard-rail tests (auto-discover all System controllers): attribute ordering, AllowAnonymous allowlist, RequirePrivilege coverage, no orphaned CertOrFIDO policies,
resource param matches method parameter, all write endpoints protected, RequiredRole enum
sync, no ExcludeFromCodeCoverage

route_judgments.rationale: Haiku cannot reliably compare a detailed spec description against a multi-file C# attribute implementation across all 4 constructor modes and 8+ guard tests.
Sonnet at medium effort can read the ticket description and the RequirePrivilege implementation to verify accuracy of each claim.
Verifying a spec description against code is a bounded review task -- Opus adds no value.

notes: Verify whether a detailed ticket description accurately describes the current RequirePrivilege implementation -- compare spec claims against code.
